  1. Part 2: Data Transformation
  2. Working with Dates and Time Series

Working with Dates and Time Series

Lesson 7

Learning Objectives

After this lesson, you will be able to:

  • Convert strings to datetime with pd.to_datetime()
  • Extract date components (year, month, day)
  • Perform date arithmetic and calculate durations
  • Work with periods and date ranges
  • Calculate rolling statistics over time

Introduction

Salary data is inherently time-based: we have snapshots at different dates, hire dates, and need to calculate tenure and salary changes over time. pandas has powerful datetime handling through the datetime64 type and the .dt accessor.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

# Create sample data with various date formats
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": ["E001", "E002", "E003", "E004", "E005"],
    "last_name": ["SMITH", "JOHNSON", "WILLIAMS", "BROWN", "JONES"],
    "hire_date_str": ["2010-08-15", "2005-01-10", "2020-08-20", "2015-06-01", "2018-09-01"],
    "snapshot_date": ["2024-01", "2024-01", "2024-01", "2024-01", "2024-01"],
    "salary": [125000, 185000, 95000, 175000, 65000]
})
df

Converting to Datetime

Using pd.to_datetime()

# Convert string to datetime
df["hire_date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["hire_date_str"])
print(df["hire_date"])
print(f"\nData type: {df['hire_date'].dtype}")
TipIn R 
# lubridate approach
library(lubridate)
df$hire_date <- ymd(df$hire_date_str)
df$hire_date <- as.Date(df$hire_date_str)

Handling Different Date Formats

pd.to_datetime() is smart about parsing, but you can specify format explicitly:

# Various formats pandas can parse automatically
dates = pd.Series([
    "2024-01-15",      # ISO format
    "January 15, 2024", # Month name
    "01/15/2024",      # US format
    "15-01-2024",      # Day first
])

# Automatic parsing (pandas guesses)
parsed = pd.to_datetime(dates)
print(parsed)
# Explicit format for better performance and control
df["snapshot"] = pd.to_datetime(df["snapshot_date"], format="%Y-%m")
df["snapshot"]

Common Format Codes

Code Meaning Example
%Y 4-digit year 2024
%y 2-digit year 24
%m Month (01-12) 01
%d Day (01-31) 15
%B Full month name January
%b Abbreviated month Jan
%H Hour (00-23) 14
%M Minute (00-59) 30

Extracting Date Components

Use the .dt accessor to extract parts of dates:

# Extract components
df["hire_year"] = df["hire_date"].dt.year
df["hire_month"] = df["hire_date"].dt.month
df["hire_day"] = df["hire_date"].dt.day
df["hire_weekday"] = df["hire_date"].dt.day_name()

df[["last_name", "hire_date", "hire_year", "hire_month", "hire_weekday"]]
TipIn R 
# lubridate
year(df$hire_date)
month(df$hire_date)
day(df$hire_date)
wday(df$hire_date, label = TRUE)

Useful .dt Attributes

# More dt attributes
print("Quarter:", df["hire_date"].dt.quarter.values)
print("Day of year:", df["hire_date"].dt.dayofyear.values)
print("Week number:", df["hire_date"].dt.isocalendar().week.values)
print("Is month end:", df["hire_date"].dt.is_month_end.values)

Date Arithmetic

Calculating Durations

# Calculate tenure in days
today = pd.Timestamp("2024-06-01")
df["tenure_days"] = (today - df["hire_date"]).dt.days

# Convert to years (approximate)
df["tenure_years"] = df["tenure_days"] / 365.25

df[["last_name", "hire_date", "tenure_days", "tenure_years"]]
TipIn R 
# lubridate
today <- as.Date("2024-06-01")
df$tenure_days <- as.numeric(today - df$hire_date)
df$tenure_years <- interval(df$hire_date, today) / years(1)

Adding/Subtracting Time

# Add time using timedelta or DateOffset
df["one_year_later"] = df["hire_date"] + pd.DateOffset(years=1)
df["six_months_later"] = df["hire_date"] + pd.DateOffset(months=6)
df["30_days_later"] = df["hire_date"] + pd.Timedelta(days=30)

df[["last_name", "hire_date", "one_year_later", "six_months_later"]]

Calculating Time Between Snapshots

# Create multi-snapshot data
snapshots = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": ["E001", "E001", "E001"],
    "snapshot": pd.to_datetime(["2023-01-01", "2023-06-01", "2024-01-01"]),
    "salary": [85000, 87000, 90000]
})

# Calculate days between snapshots
snapshots["prev_snapshot"] = snapshots.groupby("employee_id")["snapshot"].shift(1)
snapshots["days_between"] = (snapshots["snapshot"] - snapshots["prev_snapshot"]).dt.days
snapshots

Period Objects

For data like “2024-01” that represents a month (not a specific day), use Period:

# Create period from string
period = pd.Period("2024-01", freq="M")
print(f"Period: {period}")
print(f"Start: {period.start_time}")
print(f"End: {period.end_time}")
# Convert column to period
df["snapshot_period"] = df["snapshot_date"].apply(lambda x: pd.Period(x, freq="M"))
df["snapshot_period"]

Period Arithmetic

# Add months to period
period = pd.Period("2024-01", freq="M")
print(f"Current: {period}")
print(f"Next month: {period + 1}")
print(f"6 months later: {period + 6}")
print(f"Previous quarter: {period.asfreq('Q') - 1}")

Date Ranges

Generate sequences of dates:

# Daily range
daily = pd.date_range(start="2024-01-01", end="2024-01-10", freq="D")
print("Daily:", daily.tolist())
# Monthly range (first of month)
monthly = pd.date_range(start="2024-01", periods=6, freq="MS")
print("Monthly starts:", monthly.tolist())
# Business days
business = pd.date_range(start="2024-01-01", periods=5, freq="B")
print("Business days:", business.tolist())
TipIn R 
# Base R
seq(as.Date("2024-01-01"), as.Date("2024-01-10"), by = "day")

# lubridate
seq(ymd("2024-01-01"), ymd("2024-01-10"), by = "days")

Rolling Calculations

Calculate statistics over moving windows:

# Time series of salary snapshots
salary_history = pd.DataFrame({
    "date": pd.date_range("2021-01", periods=8, freq="6MS"),
    "median_salary": [75000, 76500, 78000, 79200, 80500, 82000, 84000, 86000]
})

# Rolling mean (3-period)
salary_history["rolling_mean_3"] = salary_history["median_salary"].rolling(window=3).mean()

# Rolling percent change
salary_history["pct_change"] = salary_history["median_salary"].pct_change() * 100

salary_history
TipIn R 
# dplyr/zoo
library(zoo)
df %>%
  mutate(
    rolling_mean = rollmean(salary, k = 3, fill = NA, align = "right"),
    pct_change = (salary - lag(salary)) / lag(salary) * 100
  )

Filtering by Date

# Sample data with dates
data = pd.DataFrame({
    "date": pd.date_range("2021-01-01", periods=20, freq="3MS"),
    "value": range(20)
})

# Filter to 2023 and later
recent = data[data["date"] >= "2023-01-01"]
print("2023 and later:")
print(recent)
# Filter to specific year
year_2022 = data[data["date"].dt.year == 2022]
print("\n2022 only:")
print(year_2022)
# Filter to date range
range_filter = data[(data["date"] >= "2022-06-01") & (data["date"] < "2023-06-01")]
print("\nJun 2022 to Jun 2023:")
print(range_filter)

Practical Example: Salary Tenure Analysis

# Comprehensive date analysis
employees = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": ["E001", "E002", "E003", "E004"],
    "last_name": ["SMITH", "JOHNSON", "WILLIAMS", "BROWN"],
    "hire_date": pd.to_datetime(["2010-08-15", "2005-01-10", "2020-08-20", "2015-06-01"]),
    "salary": [125000, 185000, 95000, 175000]
})

# Current date for calculations
analysis_date = pd.Timestamp("2024-06-01")

# Calculate tenure metrics
employees["tenure_years"] = ((analysis_date - employees["hire_date"]).dt.days / 365.25).round(1)
employees["hire_decade"] = (employees["hire_date"].dt.year // 10) * 10
employees["anniversary_this_year"] = employees["hire_date"].apply(
    lambda x: x.replace(year=2024) if x.month <= 6 else x.replace(year=2025)
)

# Salary per year of tenure
employees["salary_per_tenure_year"] = (employees["salary"] / employees["tenure_years"]).round(0)

employees

Quick Reference

Task pandas lubridate
Parse string pd.to_datetime(s) ymd(s), mdy(s)
Extract year dt.year year()
Extract month dt.month month()
Extract day dt.day day()
Day of week dt.day_name() wday(label=TRUE)
Add days + pd.Timedelta(days=n) + days(n)
Add months + pd.DateOffset(months=n) + months(n)
Difference in days (d1 - d2).dt.days as.numeric(d1 - d2)
Date range pd.date_range() seq()
Rolling mean rolling(n).mean() rollmean()

Exercise

Exercise 7: Analyzing Salary Trends Over Time

  1. Create a DataFrame with 5 employees and their hire dates (various years)
  2. Calculate each employee’s tenure in years as of today
  3. Group employees by the decade they were hired (2000s, 2010s, 2020s)
  4. Create a monthly date range from Jan 2023 to Dec 2024
  5. For a sample salary time series, calculate the rolling 3-month average
  6. Find employees who have their work anniversary this month
NoteSolution 
import pandas as pd
from datetime import date

# 1. Create employee data with hire dates
employees = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": ["E001", "E002", "E003", "E004", "E005"],
    "last_name": ["SMITH", "JOHNSON", "WILLIAMS", "BROWN", "JONES"],
    "hire_date": pd.to_datetime([
        "2005-03-15", "2012-07-20", "2019-11-01", "2008-06-10", "2021-02-28"
    ]),
    "salary": [125000, 95000, 78000, 110000, 72000]
})

print("1. Employee data:")
print(employees)

# 2. Calculate tenure in years
today = pd.Timestamp.today()
employees["tenure_years"] = ((today - employees["hire_date"]).dt.days / 365.25).round(1)
print("\n2. Tenure in years:")
print(employees[["last_name", "hire_date", "tenure_years"]])

# 3. Group by hire decade
employees["hire_decade"] = (employees["hire_date"].dt.year // 10) * 10
decade_summary = employees.groupby("hire_decade").agg({
    "employee_id": "count",
    "salary": "mean"
}).rename(columns={"employee_id": "count", "salary": "avg_salary"})
print("\n3. By decade hired:")
print(decade_summary)

# 4. Monthly date range
monthly_range = pd.date_range(start="2023-01", end="2024-12", freq="MS")
print("\n4. Monthly date range (first 6):")
print(monthly_range[:6].tolist())

# 5. Rolling average for salary time series
salary_trend = pd.DataFrame({
    "date": pd.date_range("2023-01", periods=12, freq="MS"),
    "median_salary": [75000, 75500, 76000, 76200, 76800, 77500,
                      78000, 78500, 79000, 79500, 80000, 80500]
})
salary_trend["rolling_3mo"] = salary_trend["median_salary"].rolling(3).mean()
print("\n5. Rolling 3-month average:")
print(salary_trend)

# 6. Employees with anniversary this month
current_month = today.month
employees["anniversary_month"] = employees["hire_date"].dt.month
anniversary_this_month = employees[employees["anniversary_month"] == current_month]
print(f"\n6. Employees with anniversary in month {current_month}:")
print(anniversary_this_month[["last_name", "hire_date"]])

Summary

You’ve learned to work with dates in pandas:

  • pd.to_datetime(): Parse strings to datetime
  • .dt accessor: Extract year, month, day, and other components
  • Date arithmetic: Calculate durations, add/subtract time
  • pd.Period: Represent time periods (months, quarters)
  • pd.date_range(): Generate date sequences
  • Rolling calculations: Moving averages and statistics

Key differences from R: - pandas uses .dt accessor instead of lubridate functions - pd.DateOffset for month/year arithmetic vs lubridate’s months()/years() - Period objects for representing time periods (no direct R equivalent)

Next Steps

Continue to Lesson 8: String Operations to learn text manipulation and cleaning.

Source Code
---
title: "Working with Dates and Time Series"
subtitle: "Lesson 7"
---

::: {.learning-objectives}
#### Learning Objectives

After this lesson, you will be able to:

- Convert strings to datetime with `pd.to_datetime()`
- Extract date components (year, month, day)
- Perform date arithmetic and calculate durations
- Work with periods and date ranges
- Calculate rolling statistics over time
:::

## Introduction

Salary data is inherently time-based: we have snapshots at different dates, hire dates, and need to calculate tenure and salary changes over time. pandas has powerful datetime handling through the `datetime64` type and the `.dt` accessor.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

# Create sample data with various date formats
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": ["E001", "E002", "E003", "E004", "E005"],
    "last_name": ["SMITH", "JOHNSON", "WILLIAMS", "BROWN", "JONES"],
    "hire_date_str": ["2010-08-15", "2005-01-10", "2020-08-20", "2015-06-01", "2018-09-01"],
    "snapshot_date": ["2024-01", "2024-01", "2024-01", "2024-01", "2024-01"],
    "salary": [125000, 185000, 95000, 175000, 65000]
})
df
```

## Converting to Datetime

### Using `pd.to_datetime()`

```{python}
# Convert string to datetime
df["hire_date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["hire_date_str"])
print(df["hire_date"])
print(f"\nData type: {df['hire_date'].dtype}")
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## In R

```r
# lubridate approach
library(lubridate)
df$hire_date <- ymd(df$hire_date_str)
df$hire_date <- as.Date(df$hire_date_str)
```
:::

### Handling Different Date Formats

`pd.to_datetime()` is smart about parsing, but you can specify format explicitly:

```{python}
# Various formats pandas can parse automatically
dates = pd.Series([
    "2024-01-15",      # ISO format
    "January 15, 2024", # Month name
    "01/15/2024",      # US format
    "15-01-2024",      # Day first
])

# Automatic parsing (pandas guesses)
parsed = pd.to_datetime(dates)
print(parsed)
```

```{python}
# Explicit format for better performance and control
df["snapshot"] = pd.to_datetime(df["snapshot_date"], format="%Y-%m")
df["snapshot"]
```

### Common Format Codes

| Code | Meaning | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| `%Y` | 4-digit year | 2024 |
| `%y` | 2-digit year | 24 |
| `%m` | Month (01-12) | 01 |
| `%d` | Day (01-31) | 15 |
| `%B` | Full month name | January |
| `%b` | Abbreviated month | Jan |
| `%H` | Hour (00-23) | 14 |
| `%M` | Minute (00-59) | 30 |

## Extracting Date Components

Use the `.dt` accessor to extract parts of dates:

```{python}
# Extract components
df["hire_year"] = df["hire_date"].dt.year
df["hire_month"] = df["hire_date"].dt.month
df["hire_day"] = df["hire_date"].dt.day
df["hire_weekday"] = df["hire_date"].dt.day_name()

df[["last_name", "hire_date", "hire_year", "hire_month", "hire_weekday"]]
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## In R

```r
# lubridate
year(df$hire_date)
month(df$hire_date)
day(df$hire_date)
wday(df$hire_date, label = TRUE)
```
:::

### Useful `.dt` Attributes

```{python}
# More dt attributes
print("Quarter:", df["hire_date"].dt.quarter.values)
print("Day of year:", df["hire_date"].dt.dayofyear.values)
print("Week number:", df["hire_date"].dt.isocalendar().week.values)
print("Is month end:", df["hire_date"].dt.is_month_end.values)
```

## Date Arithmetic

### Calculating Durations

```{python}
# Calculate tenure in days
today = pd.Timestamp("2024-06-01")
df["tenure_days"] = (today - df["hire_date"]).dt.days

# Convert to years (approximate)
df["tenure_years"] = df["tenure_days"] / 365.25

df[["last_name", "hire_date", "tenure_days", "tenure_years"]]
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## In R

```r
# lubridate
today <- as.Date("2024-06-01")
df$tenure_days <- as.numeric(today - df$hire_date)
df$tenure_years <- interval(df$hire_date, today) / years(1)
```
:::

### Adding/Subtracting Time

```{python}
# Add time using timedelta or DateOffset
df["one_year_later"] = df["hire_date"] + pd.DateOffset(years=1)
df["six_months_later"] = df["hire_date"] + pd.DateOffset(months=6)
df["30_days_later"] = df["hire_date"] + pd.Timedelta(days=30)

df[["last_name", "hire_date", "one_year_later", "six_months_later"]]
```

### Calculating Time Between Snapshots

```{python}
# Create multi-snapshot data
snapshots = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": ["E001", "E001", "E001"],
    "snapshot": pd.to_datetime(["2023-01-01", "2023-06-01", "2024-01-01"]),
    "salary": [85000, 87000, 90000]
})

# Calculate days between snapshots
snapshots["prev_snapshot"] = snapshots.groupby("employee_id")["snapshot"].shift(1)
snapshots["days_between"] = (snapshots["snapshot"] - snapshots["prev_snapshot"]).dt.days
snapshots
```

## Period Objects

For data like "2024-01" that represents a month (not a specific day), use `Period`:

```{python}
# Create period from string
period = pd.Period("2024-01", freq="M")
print(f"Period: {period}")
print(f"Start: {period.start_time}")
print(f"End: {period.end_time}")
```

```{python}
# Convert column to period
df["snapshot_period"] = df["snapshot_date"].apply(lambda x: pd.Period(x, freq="M"))
df["snapshot_period"]
```

### Period Arithmetic

```{python}
# Add months to period
period = pd.Period("2024-01", freq="M")
print(f"Current: {period}")
print(f"Next month: {period + 1}")
print(f"6 months later: {period + 6}")
print(f"Previous quarter: {period.asfreq('Q') - 1}")
```

## Date Ranges

Generate sequences of dates:

```{python}
# Daily range
daily = pd.date_range(start="2024-01-01", end="2024-01-10", freq="D")
print("Daily:", daily.tolist())
```

```{python}
# Monthly range (first of month)
monthly = pd.date_range(start="2024-01", periods=6, freq="MS")
print("Monthly starts:", monthly.tolist())
```

```{python}
# Business days
business = pd.date_range(start="2024-01-01", periods=5, freq="B")
print("Business days:", business.tolist())
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## In R

```r
# Base R
seq(as.Date("2024-01-01"), as.Date("2024-01-10"), by = "day")

# lubridate
seq(ymd("2024-01-01"), ymd("2024-01-10"), by = "days")
```
:::

## Rolling Calculations

Calculate statistics over moving windows:

```{python}
# Time series of salary snapshots
salary_history = pd.DataFrame({
    "date": pd.date_range("2021-01", periods=8, freq="6MS"),
    "median_salary": [75000, 76500, 78000, 79200, 80500, 82000, 84000, 86000]
})

# Rolling mean (3-period)
salary_history["rolling_mean_3"] = salary_history["median_salary"].rolling(window=3).mean()

# Rolling percent change
salary_history["pct_change"] = salary_history["median_salary"].pct_change() * 100

salary_history
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## In R

```r
# dplyr/zoo
library(zoo)
df %>%
  mutate(
    rolling_mean = rollmean(salary, k = 3, fill = NA, align = "right"),
    pct_change = (salary - lag(salary)) / lag(salary) * 100
  )
```
:::

## Filtering by Date

```{python}
# Sample data with dates
data = pd.DataFrame({
    "date": pd.date_range("2021-01-01", periods=20, freq="3MS"),
    "value": range(20)
})

# Filter to 2023 and later
recent = data[data["date"] >= "2023-01-01"]
print("2023 and later:")
print(recent)
```

```{python}
# Filter to specific year
year_2022 = data[data["date"].dt.year == 2022]
print("\n2022 only:")
print(year_2022)
```

```{python}
# Filter to date range
range_filter = data[(data["date"] >= "2022-06-01") & (data["date"] < "2023-06-01")]
print("\nJun 2022 to Jun 2023:")
print(range_filter)
```

## Practical Example: Salary Tenure Analysis

```{python}
# Comprehensive date analysis
employees = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": ["E001", "E002", "E003", "E004"],
    "last_name": ["SMITH", "JOHNSON", "WILLIAMS", "BROWN"],
    "hire_date": pd.to_datetime(["2010-08-15", "2005-01-10", "2020-08-20", "2015-06-01"]),
    "salary": [125000, 185000, 95000, 175000]
})

# Current date for calculations
analysis_date = pd.Timestamp("2024-06-01")

# Calculate tenure metrics
employees["tenure_years"] = ((analysis_date - employees["hire_date"]).dt.days / 365.25).round(1)
employees["hire_decade"] = (employees["hire_date"].dt.year // 10) * 10
employees["anniversary_this_year"] = employees["hire_date"].apply(
    lambda x: x.replace(year=2024) if x.month <= 6 else x.replace(year=2025)
)

# Salary per year of tenure
employees["salary_per_tenure_year"] = (employees["salary"] / employees["tenure_years"]).round(0)

employees
```

## Quick Reference

| Task | pandas | lubridate |
|------|--------|-----------|
| Parse string | `pd.to_datetime(s)` | `ymd(s)`, `mdy(s)` |
| Extract year | `dt.year` | `year()` |
| Extract month | `dt.month` | `month()` |
| Extract day | `dt.day` | `day()` |
| Day of week | `dt.day_name()` | `wday(label=TRUE)` |
| Add days | `+ pd.Timedelta(days=n)` | `+ days(n)` |
| Add months | `+ pd.DateOffset(months=n)` | `+ months(n)` |
| Difference in days | `(d1 - d2).dt.days` | `as.numeric(d1 - d2)` |
| Date range | `pd.date_range()` | `seq()` |
| Rolling mean | `rolling(n).mean()` | `rollmean()` |

## Exercise {#exercise}

::: {.exercise-box}
#### Exercise 7: Analyzing Salary Trends Over Time

1. Create a DataFrame with 5 employees and their hire dates (various years)
2. Calculate each employee's tenure in years as of today
3. Group employees by the decade they were hired (2000s, 2010s, 2020s)
4. Create a monthly date range from Jan 2023 to Dec 2024
5. For a sample salary time series, calculate the rolling 3-month average
6. Find employees who have their work anniversary this month

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Solution

```{python}
import pandas as pd
from datetime import date

# 1. Create employee data with hire dates
employees = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee_id": ["E001", "E002", "E003", "E004", "E005"],
    "last_name": ["SMITH", "JOHNSON", "WILLIAMS", "BROWN", "JONES"],
    "hire_date": pd.to_datetime([
        "2005-03-15", "2012-07-20", "2019-11-01", "2008-06-10", "2021-02-28"
    ]),
    "salary": [125000, 95000, 78000, 110000, 72000]
})

print("1. Employee data:")
print(employees)

# 2. Calculate tenure in years
today = pd.Timestamp.today()
employees["tenure_years"] = ((today - employees["hire_date"]).dt.days / 365.25).round(1)
print("\n2. Tenure in years:")
print(employees[["last_name", "hire_date", "tenure_years"]])

# 3. Group by hire decade
employees["hire_decade"] = (employees["hire_date"].dt.year // 10) * 10
decade_summary = employees.groupby("hire_decade").agg({
    "employee_id": "count",
    "salary": "mean"
}).rename(columns={"employee_id": "count", "salary": "avg_salary"})
print("\n3. By decade hired:")
print(decade_summary)

# 4. Monthly date range
monthly_range = pd.date_range(start="2023-01", end="2024-12", freq="MS")
print("\n4. Monthly date range (first 6):")
print(monthly_range[:6].tolist())

# 5. Rolling average for salary time series
salary_trend = pd.DataFrame({
    "date": pd.date_range("2023-01", periods=12, freq="MS"),
    "median_salary": [75000, 75500, 76000, 76200, 76800, 77500,
                      78000, 78500, 79000, 79500, 80000, 80500]
})
salary_trend["rolling_3mo"] = salary_trend["median_salary"].rolling(3).mean()
print("\n5. Rolling 3-month average:")
print(salary_trend)

# 6. Employees with anniversary this month
current_month = today.month
employees["anniversary_month"] = employees["hire_date"].dt.month
anniversary_this_month = employees[employees["anniversary_month"] == current_month]
print(f"\n6. Employees with anniversary in month {current_month}:")
print(anniversary_this_month[["last_name", "hire_date"]])
```
:::
:::

## Summary

You've learned to work with dates in pandas:

- **`pd.to_datetime()`**: Parse strings to datetime
- **`.dt` accessor**: Extract year, month, day, and other components
- **Date arithmetic**: Calculate durations, add/subtract time
- **`pd.Period`**: Represent time periods (months, quarters)
- **`pd.date_range()`**: Generate date sequences
- **Rolling calculations**: Moving averages and statistics

Key differences from R:
- pandas uses `.dt` accessor instead of lubridate functions
- `pd.DateOffset` for month/year arithmetic vs lubridate's `months()`/`years()`
- `Period` objects for representing time periods (no direct R equivalent)

## Next Steps

Continue to [Lesson 8: String Operations](08-string-operations.qmd) to learn text manipulation and cleaning.
 

Built with Quarto | Data: UW-Madison Public Salary Records
